title: 2026/04/22 - EW [Part 2]
displayTitle: Education and Workforce Committee - Employment Leave Bill [Part 2]
description: Wednesday 22 April 2026 | Education and Workforce Committee Employment Leave Bill

chunks.text: And Michael Kilkelly joining us. Get set. Karen, we have 10 minutes with you this morning. That includes time for questions. Pass it over to you now. Thank you. Mauri ora koutou. We're both members of the National Employment Law Committee of the New Zealand Law Society, Te Kāhui Ture o Aotearoa.

I'm a barrister in Wellington. I've got over 30 years experience in employment law, which covers two Holidays Acts and two decades in large law firms. And Michael is an associate with Meredith Connell in Auckland, and prior to that he was a judge's clerk in the Employment Court in Auckland. The approach taken by the New Zealand Law Society when commenting on proposed legislation is a neutral one in terms of policy.

So we're seeking to provide you with feedback on the workability of the legislation, identifying potential issues in drafting. And as lawyers, our natural review mechanism is to think about how we might explain it to our clients. Mm-hmm. Our written submission makes a number of comments that are directed to the predictability and certainty of the proposed legislation, but we'll focus only on three of those points today, and then leave a few minutes for questions from you.

So I'll start with the standard hours point that we make in our submission. The bill is premised on standard hours being specified in an employment agreement roster or notional roster, and that's the baseline or foundation for annual leave, public holidays, bereavement leave, sick leave, domestic violence leave, et cetera.

So it's really important as a concept in this draft legislation. So just to start with a practical issue. At the moment, standard hours isn't required in employment agreements. The Employment Relations Act brings in more flexibility than that, and it refers to a requirement to include an indication of the arrangements relating to the times the employee is to work. So you see some quite flexible and fluid clauses at the moment in employment agreements.

So there's going to be some transition needed if we go with the standard hours notion from what currently is quite flexible and where many employment agreements won't have standard or set hours specified, and it's not quite clear how that transition's going to be addressed. This leads to the second issue about standard hours, which is the bill requires that, but doesn't provide a fallback mechanism if the hours aren't specified in any of those ways. So where the employer and employee simply don't agree and they stick with

their current employment agreement, what happens then? The third issue is whether the standard hours truly reflect what the employee works. And I listened earlier this morning to some of the industry submissions, which have explained the implication of that for how the time off part of leave is calculated.

For the Law Society, our concern is twofold. First is that the standard hours might not reflect the current reality, but second, the, shall we say, less honorable employers might deliberately set low standard hours knowing that the employees will happily voluntarily work additional hours- Mm-hmm... in order to boost their pay to that point.

But that means they don't have annual leave and the other actual time off entitlements that accrue at the rate that they're actually working. The second point I'm going to touch on is what we colloquially know as time and a half, for payment on public holidays. If I can be frank, the clause relating to that, 124, it's quite long. It has cross-references in itself, and references between some clauses that are quite difficult to follow.

It's one of those clauses that you read and you wonder how you might actually explain that to anybody in terms of how it's meant to work. So it is complicated. The second point about it is that there are two mechanisms for the time and a half. So there's one for time, and that's what the employer's required to pay the employee for each hour worked. And then the half has a different calculation, and that's greater of 50% of the employee's ordinary hourly rate and any identifiable amount.

So you're doing one calculation for the time and then another one for the half. And I'm by no means a payroll expert, but I think that's going to complicate things as people try to put this in practice.So that, yeah, those calculation parts might not be the same, which risks the calculations being very complex to do.

So I'll hand over to Michael now to talk about our final point, which is the remediation issue. Thank you, Karen, and good morning, everyone. So I'm just going to speak to you briefly about the proposed remediation process for outstanding liabilities under the Holidays Act, and those provisions are at Clause 156 in Schedule 3 of the bill.

It's proposed that that process will have retrospective effect and could be used to remediate underpayments and payment failures, which, for whatever reason, have occurred up to four years prior to the bill receiving the Royal Assent. We're concerned that the material relating to the bill does not discuss whether the retrospective provisions are appropriate.

There does not seem to be any consideration of whether retrospectivity can adversely impact either employers or employees. For example, by reducing or extinguishing some employees' statutory entitlements. Second, whether the extent of the retrospectivity is appropriate or if it requires adjustment, and third, whether this process could upset or lead to relitigation of settled matters.

We also have concerns as to whether it is appropriate to prescribe the substantive content of those retrospective provisions through regulations rather than through primary legislation, because regulations would not be subject to the same level of public and parliamentary scrutiny.

Those concerns are heightened as the bill does not require the minister to undertake public consultation on the draft regulations in circumstances where statutory entitlements are impacted. Finally, we have concerns about regulations that would specify a minimum amount payable, a term that is in that undefined category that I just spoke about, will be defined through the regulations, because the regulations would not be subject to the same level of parliamentary and public scrutiny.

This would mean an employer would not be required to make a remediation payment to an employee whose entitlement is below this threshold. It raises fairness concerns as the threshold could bar an employee from receiving a payment they are otherwise entitled to receive, no matter how small the amount. Those concerns are heightened because we do not know at what level that threshold will be set or how it will be calculated.

And a similar concern arises around the concept of reasonable compensation, given that also appears to contemplate something less than their existing statutory entitlements. We advise you to seek advice on those matters and to consider whether amendments are necessary to address those concerns.

I'll hand over for questions if there's any time. Thank you very much. We do have a short amount of time, but we do have one question. I was going to say, it doesn't appear we have any. We have a question from Jan Tinetti. Thank you. Thank you, Madam Chair, and thank you very much for your submission. It kind of backs up, in many respects, what some of the industry bodies have been saying, as you said.

Just one thing I want to get there, you talked about if there is not so savory employers as such and what they might do in this situation. To put it in simple terms, would that be... And I'm trying to get my head around this too. Does that mean that they could be incentivizing part-time and casualization workers in this particular case because it could mean that they're paying out less in the annual leave?

To answer that vaguely, yes, possibly. But also you could have full-time employees who are put on, say, 35 or 30 hours a week. It may be that you're thinking of that as part-time, which is probably right, but work greater hours than that. So they might, say, have the standard hours of 30 or 35 in their agreement, but work much more than that and on a regular basis.

And I think there is a possibility of some, and I'm hesitating to use this word, but some abuse of the system, particularly by unscrupulous employers who are trying to limit how much time off people actually have, particularly if they have to bring in replacement employees at a higher cost.

Great. Thank you. Thank you for that. And a question from Lawrence Yunane. Thank you, Madam Chair. Just a very quick question regarding the standard hours as well. Do you have any data or what is your understanding of the current average hours that employees actually work a week?

And potentially, is it greater than the standard hours that we're seeing now? The reason I ask this question is, we hear anecdotally, but I want to check if there's any data to say that people are working longer and longer hours that are unseen or unaccounted for in their contract.

That's not data that the Law Society would hold. So the answer to your question is no, we don't have that data. It might be held by the Victoria University Employment Relations Centre. They do an annual analysis of collective agreements and what provisions are in those. So the data may be somewhere, but it's not held by us, I'm afraid.

Thank you. We might look into that. Thank you so much. All right. Thank you very much. We appreciate your participation in the process and your very constructive and helpful submission. So thank you again. Thank you. We'll move on now to of a similar theme, Simpson Grierson. We have Rachel Judge and Mike Mercer joining us.

Rachel and Mike, we have 10 minutes with you this morning, and that includes time for questions. Thank you. Tena koutou katoa. I am Rachel Judge, a partner at Simpson Grierson, and I'm presenting with my colleague, Mike Mercer, Senior Associate. We have both been heavily involved in advising on the current Holidays Act and assisting clients through their remediation processes.

And we thank you for the opportunity to present our oral submission today on behalf of Simpson Grierson. This submission has been informed by feedback from a number of our clients and also, in line with what Karen's just spoken to, our own observations as lawyers as to where complexities may arise based on the wording of the current bill.

At the outset, I do want to note that Simpson Grierson supports the core objectives of the bill. We have been actively advocating for many of these changes for a number of years. In particular, the move to an hours-based accrual model and the simplification of leave payment calculations. That said, there are some aspects of the bill that are likely to pose some compliance issues for employers, and particularly those with complex workforces. So we do consider that there is room for the Bill to potentially include some options to

address some of those issues. And we wish to comment on three key issues from our written submissions where we have made recommendations. Those are the notional roster, the mandatory standard hours versus additional hours leave compensation payment approach, which we'll refer to as LCP, and the issue of strike action during the transitional period.

So just turning to the notional roster framework to start with. This applies to determine leave entitlements where an employee's standard hours or days of work cannot be readily identified. And while the policy intent is understood, we submit that the framework as drafted may not be workable in practice for many employers. Clause nine, for example, requires that a notional roster is agreed in writing and kept up to date. In workplaces where hours are variable or change frequently, that will be difficult, if not impossible, to apply.

The combination of a requirement for agreement and then continual updating risks continuous negotiation, creating significant administrative burden and also employee relations issues flowing from all of that. For large employers, and particularly those with unionized workforces, this is unlikely to be practicable.

There is also a level of uncertainty in the role a notional roster is intended to play. Although it's described as notional, the provisions do require it to reflect actual working patterns to a certain extent and to be revised as those patterns change. So this risks blurring the distinction between a genuine notional construct and an operational roster, and in our view, risks undermining the purpose of introducing a simplified mechanism in the first place.

We recognize that there is provision at clauses 10 and 11 for a labor inspector to determine a notional roster where agreement cannot be reached, but we consider that reliance on labor inspector determinations as the sole fallback is likely to result in increased disputes, delay, and litigation. So on this point, we submit that the framework would be materially improved by introducing an express alternative mechanism where clause nine could be amended to provide that where it is not reasonably practicable for an employer and employee to

agree a notional roster. That agreement is not required as a precondition to determining those standard hours for leave purposes. And in line with that, that clause 10 is amended to allow an employer, without labor inspector intervention, to determine a notional roster by applying some form of agreed averaging method. And we're hearing from our clients that if some form of rolling average were applied, such as the 13-week average, that that's something that could be workable for them going forward.

Mm-hmm. I'll now hand over to Mike Mercer to discuss the further two points we wanted to raise. Thank you, Rachel, and kia ora koutou katoa. Further, the two points I want to speak to, the first relates to the leave compensation approach, and like our learned friends from the New Zealand Law Society, we address the mechanism for additional hours being paid with via the leave compensation payment.

While we understand this is intended to provide certainty and to avoid the accumulation of large leave liabilities, there are circumstances where hours of work will fluctuate and it could create some outcomes for an employer that are not consistent with the Bill and operational difficulties. In particular, the structure of the leave compensation payment will create incentive for employees to favor being paid in a cash loading rather than accruing leave and resist agreement to standard hours.

What we're hearing from clients who are really concerned about their ability to agree with standard hours with their employees is that given the attractiveness of leave compensation payment, you could end up in a situation that is different to what was being discussed previously, with actually employees, all time including, who would prefer to end up on low standard hours with large additional hours that would provide the disincentive to them taking sick leave when needed and significantly receive their reduced pay when they do

so.The bill doesn't currently allow for alternative approaches that may produce the same or better outcome for employees. In practice, we are hearing from clients who have a strong preference to accrue leave on all hours worked, including additional hours, while others may prefer the pay-as-you-go model of the compensation payment.

Either approach would be broadly neutral in effect for employees. They either get leave paid at the time or the ability to take it later. In our view, there is scope to deliver on the intent of the bill while allowing greater flexibility in how leave entitlements are delivered without jeopardizing employee protections or entitlements. We therefore recommend that clauses 129 and 132 of the bill be amended to allow employers, as an alternative to paying leave compensation on additional hours, to accrue leave on all hours worked in addition,

including those additional hours at their election. The second point we wish to raise relates to strike action during the transitional period. It is inevitable that there will be areas of disagreement during the transition process, given the new leave framework will require significant changes to existing employment agreements, payroll systems, and operational practices.

We are concerned that without clear limits, disputes arising solely from the application of the transitional provisions may become the subject of strike action or lockouts. There is precedent that unions and employees cannot participate in unlawful industrial action, even when it otherwise relates to bargaining, if the issue in dispute arises from a legislatively defined process.

By way of example, the Fair Pay Agreements Act in 2022, which has since been repealed, included express restrictions on industrial action in relation to fair pay bargaining. We therefore recommend that the bill be amended to provide that disputes arising solely from the application or operation of the transitional provisions cannot be subject to industrial action during the transitional period. This could be achieved by either adopting a similar approach to that in the former Fair Pay Agreement Act, and in this context, disagreements about the conversion, reconciliation, or technical

application of the transition rules should be treated as procedural matters and resolved through interpretation, guidance, or dispute resolution mechanisms by the employment institutions rather than industrial action. Thank you, Madam Chair and members of the Select Committee. We are prepared to take any questions at this time.

We have one minute, and it doesn't appear that we do. And can I just say, it is not a reflection of the lack of interest. I have to make that very clear. Very good. You always put forward an incredibly- Very good... comprehensive and helpful submission. Yeah. And I thank you for that, and I know that our officials would- Excellent. Thank you... thank you for that, too. Thank you. Very good. Thank you. Thank you.

So we'll move on now to our next submitter. Thank you. Thank you. We have Madfly Limited Trading. We have Sheryn Waltu. Hopefully, I'm pronouncing your surname correctly. That's all right. Enterprise and Staffing Innovations New Zealand, if that makes more sense to you.

Apologies. Having a few technical issues there. I hope you can hear me. That's okay. Easy. You got- Right. So apologies. EasyNZ, sorry. Let's just get that right. I happen to see in your logo. Sorry. It is actually, it's a trading name of Madfly Limited. So, thank you, Madam Chairman and members of the committee. Thank you for the opportunity to appear here today and to speak on, uh, to EasyNZ's written submission on the Employment Leave Bill.

My name is Sheryn Waltu, and I'm the managing director appearing on behalf of Enterprise and Staffing Innovations NZ, otherwise known as EasyNZ. By way of background, EasyNZ provides practical employment relations and HR support to businesses across New Zealand. A big part of our work is helping clients with the operational employment issues that come up every day in real workplaces.

Things like leave, payroll, payroll interpretation, rostering process, and documentation. These are matters that can look quite straightforward on paper, but if they're handled incorrectly, they can very quickly turn into disputes. A large proportion of our clients are small to medium-sized businesses. For those businesses, getting employment processes wrong is not just frustrating, it's expensive, distracting, and disruptive.

Most do not have in-house legal teams or large HR functions, if at all. They're trying to do the right thing, but they need the law to be clear, and they need it to be workable. When disputes do arise, even a claim with little or no merit can still cost up to $10,000 to defend in cost alone before you factor in management time, stress, and disruption to the business.

So from our perspective, clarity in employment legislation really matters. If the law is too technical, too uncertain, or too hard to apply in day-to-day operations, the consequences are very real. With that in mind, our overall position on this is that we support the direction of reform, but we say the bill still needs refinement. We support the objective behind the bill.

The current leave framework is too complicated. It's been difficult for employers to understand and difficult for payroll systems to properly apply, and it's led to too many errors and too much uncertainty, so reform is needed. We support the intention of creating a simpler and clearer leave system, but our key message today is that simplification should not come at the cost of fairness, operational workability, or new compliance risks.

In our view, the bill makes some very positive changes, but there are still several areas where it needs tightening so it does not simply replace one set of problems with another. So there are parts of the bill that we clearly support. We support pro-rated sick leave accruing from the first day of employment. That's easier to understand, and it better reflects service over time. We support annual leave accruing from day one.

That's much easier to explain than the current distinction between accrued leave and annual leave becoming available only after 12 months. And we support clearer rules around public holidays and otherwise working days. Greater clarity in that area should reduce uncertainty and reduce the risk of underpayment issues.So this is not a submission opposing reform, quite the opposite. We support reform.

We just want to make sure the reform is genuinely workable in practice. Our biggest concern is the 12.5% leave compensation payment for hours above minimum contracted hours. Mm. This is probably our most significant concern. We're concerned about the proposal to require a leave compensation payment of at least 12.5% for additional hours and casual hours.

That may appear simple at a high level, but in practice, it creates real problems for businesses where workers have genuine ongoing employment and regular patterns of work, but may work more than their guaranteed minimum hours. This is a very common arrangement, especially in small to medium businesses. In many cases, businesses will guarantee, for example, 30 hours a week, but regularly offer more. The reason for that is practical.

In sectors such as agriculture and construction, the amount of work available is often heavily dependent on weather conditions and changing operational demands. Businesses are trying to provide employees with a reliable minimum, while still retaining the flexibility to offer more hours when conditions allow. So a person may be contracted for 30 hours, but often work 40 when the weather is favorable and work can proceed, and in things like construction, that could actually be even more, so you might work more hours in the summer than you do in the winter.

That does not make them casual in the ordinary sense. Our concern is that if every hour above guaranteed minimum automatically attracts a 12.5% leave payment, that creates a number of problems. So it creates more payroll complexity, not less. It increases labor cost immediately.

It may reduce the employee's ability to build up meaningful paid leave over time, and it may encourage a system where leave is effectively cashed out as work happens, instead of being preserved as time away from work. From our perspective, that's not just a payroll issue, it's also a wellbeing issue. Mm. So our recommendation is that the bill should draw a clearer distinction between genuine casual work and ordinary additional hours worked in ongoing employment.

Where there is a real ongoing employment, there should be a pathway for leave to accrue rather than requiring automatic pay-as-you-go treatment. So the bill also needs to be clearer and more practical, there needs to be more practical treatment of casual employment. Closely linked to the 12.5% leave compensation payment is the question of casual work.

One of the real issues for employers is knowing when a worker who starts out as a casual has in reality become regular. Mm. That matters because it affects payroll treatment, leave treatment, legal risks, and fairness. If the bill is going to rely on a separate model for casual employees, then it needs to be much clearer about when someone is genuinely casual and when they're no longer casual.

Mm. Otherwise, we're just creating a new uncertainty in place of an old one. So our recommendation is that the bill include either a clearer statutory test or a review point, so that where work becomes regular and predictable, the employee moves into the ordinary accrual work frame or framework, sorry. The bill should also avoid creating unfairness between casual and permanent workers. We're also concerned about parity on this one.

If a casual worker receives 12.5% upfront, but a permanent worker accrues leave that only becomes valuable when it's actually taken, then there's a real possibility of inequitable outcomes between different categories of workers. We're not suggesting casual workers should be disadvantaged. That's not our point. Our point is that simplification should still produce fair outcomes across the workforce. The committee should test carefully whether the structure of the bill creates unintended imbalance between workers who may be

doing similar work, but under different employment arrangements. So next we have KiwiSaver and commission need to be expressly clarified. We also think the bill should be clearer on some points around the remuneration issues. The first is KiwiSaver. The treatment of KiwiSaver in relation to leave compensation payments should be set out clearly in the legislation.

Mm. If it's not, employers and payroll providers will take different approaches, and that inconsistency will create exactly the kind of confusion this reform is trying to fix. The second is commission and non-discretionary incentive payments. A simpler model, sorry, simpler leave model should not result in regular commission income being left out of leave calculations where that income is a normal part of the employee's earnings. If that happens, employees in commission-based or incentive-based roles may end up worse off

when taking leave, and that creates a real disincentive to taking leave at all. That would be an unfair outcome and would undermine the broader purpose of leave entitlements. So we say the bill should expressly preserve the inclusion of regular non-discretionary payment and similar payments, or provide a mechanism that properly captures them.

The variable hour work is still not fully resolved, especially around day-based leave and notional rosters. Our final point is that the bill does not yet fully resolve the practical issues that arise for workers with variable hours and variable days. Mm. We remain concerned about day-based leave, particularly bereavement leave and family violence leave.

Mm. Where people do not work the same pattern each week, there can be uncertainty about what counts as a day, how part days should be treated, and how leave should apply across changing rosters. We're also concerned about notional rosters. At the moment, the bill does not seem to give enough practical certainty about when a notional roster is required, what information is needed to create one, and when it should be reviewed.

Without that clarity, there's a real risk of more inconsistency, more confusion, and ultimately more disputes. At the very least, we think that the legislation or supporting regulations should include clearer guidance and practical worked examples before the bill comes into force. So in closing, our message is a straightforward one.

We support the goal of this bill. We support day one accrual for sick leave and annual leave, and we support clearer public holiday rules. But we ask the committee to refine the bill so it does not simplify shift complexity from one part of the system to another. Sorry, does not simply shift complexity. Sorry. In particular, we ask the committee to look carefully at the 12.5% leave compensation rule, the treatment of casual work, fairness between casual and permanent workers, KiwiSaver treatment, commissioner and incentive payments, and the practical operation of day-based

leave and notional rosters. If these areas are improved, we think the bill will be stronger, fairer, and much more workable in the real world. Thank you for your time, and I'm happy to answer questions if you've got time. Thank you very much, Sherylyn. We do believe-- I think we've run out of time with you, we have, unfortunately. But thank you so much for being part of this process, and I've got to say, a really good submission, really helpful.
And I assume- Thank you... and assure you, sorry, that the officials will be pleased too. Oh, thank you very much. Move on now to Essential HR. Mike Johnson, who I believe is online.

Mike, there we are. Wonderful. Oh. I came, and I went. Yes. That was a quick submission. There we go, Mike. Ten minutes for you, includes time for questions, and we'll hand it over to you now. Tena koutou. Good morning. I'm Mike Johnson from Essential HR, and I want to thank you for giving me an opportunity to speak to you all.

Madam Chairman, and members of the committee, I'd like to start by saying how impressed I am with the draft legislation. It had a horrible starting place, being the Holidays Act, and so many things to address, and it does, from my point of view, I really sense some positive development moving ahead from the Holidays Act, and a very clear attempt to address a wide range of issues, things like casual staff and the pro-rata-ing of sick leave from day one, dealing with on-call and stuff like that. It's, yeah, raise my hat

to you all, and especially as you now try and process everything that's coming at you in this consultation. Now, I speak on behalf of SMEs. I have some four to five hundred whom I provide HR advice to, and I've been pleased that I can already go back to them and confirm for many of them that their concerns will be addressed in the new legislation, and there'll always be some, of course, that are not.

There is one, though, in particular, and it's the first and significant issue that I referred to in my written submission, that I want to address today, and I strongly suggest you consider the particular recommendation that I'm going to make. And the reality is that this is all about enabling small businesses to comply with the legislation.

So I want you to start by picturing an SME. It may be a small retail outlet, a cafe, a restaurant, roofing contractor. There are any number of them whose workload is dependent on the weather or on a daily varying level of customer demand. We all know them, and these businesses can thrive, but that's especially where staff are working as a team. They dig deep sometimes.

They leave early where that benefits the business. They'll work a Saturday to help make up time. And for these and many others, compliance with the Holidays Act was just a nightmare. There was no way a business owner could understand what was needed to comply, so they relied on their payroll provider to do things right. But we all know that at some stage, all payroll systems were non-compliant with the Holidays Act.

And yet it was the SME who bore the brunt and the cost of a labor inspector's audit and its resulting penalties, and it just didn't seem right. For example, how many payroll systems that you know, do you think would cope easily with a roster that has somebody working, Wednesday, seven hours, Thursday, 10, Friday, 10, Saturday, 14 and a half, Sunday, two, with sleepovers in between each of them, then three days rest, and then going through the whole thing again. It was a roster that effectively is an eight-day revolving roster, but payroll systems just

couldn't cope with it. It was almost not possible to comply with the legislation. We need something similar, something simple rather, not similar, something simple to manage what have become some of the most complex of rosters. And while this bill has gone so far, it can easily go a step further in removing complexity.

It's a real challenge when it comes to drafting legislation, I know. And I've just pulled out Section 123 of the bill, Section 2, Subsection 2, where "The employee's leave payment must not be not less than the rate of leave payment for each relevant hour, that the employee takes as a period of leave, or that he does not work on a public holiday that would otherwise be a working day," et cetera.

Yet for most business owners, if you present them with that, it's not that it's wrong. It's not. It's correct. But it's just that it leaves them saying, "Eh?" The business owners are never going to get their heads around it. For the SMEs that I had you picturing before, there's no simple standard hours, and the many additional hours may or may not be linked to an availability clause, because for many of them, it's actually just part of their commitment to their jobs.

So we need a simpler way to collate dollars for leave payments broadly, and for ease of understanding and to avoid having to rely on the payroll provider for compliance. So please, can we have just a few very simple rules built into this legislation?

The first one, for every dollar that you earn-You put aside, or that somebody earns, you put aside another 12 and a half cents for leave, eight and a half probably for annual leave, four for sick leave. For every hour worked, you accrue the 0.0769 hours plus 0.0385 hours.

For every uncertain otherwise working day, we use the 13 weeks. It is great. You've got some very simple stuff already built into the draft legislation. Take it that step further, that all earnings get treated the same way. You don't separate out standard hours from other hours.

That everything that relates to hours worked or protection of employment or employment status, leave that with the Employment Relations Act. We don't need to muddy the waters within the Employment Leave Act. And leave it so that payroll providers are doing just that. They're providing a payroll service.

I really urge you to press on. Don't allow the tightness of the timeframe to stop you from making that extra change and making it that little bit more simple that employers will be able to comply. Are there any questions? Thank you, Mike. I don't appear to have any questions.

Oh, we have one from Pam Shea. Very quickly, Pam. Just very quickly, Mike, thank you so much. When you started your submission, Mike, you sounded like you're fully supportive of this legislation. And then, of course, you want this legislation to go a bit further. Then when I read your first line in your submission, it says that you're neither supporting nor opposing this bill. So why so undecided? Okay. Thank you for pointing that out.

I tend to start most of my submissions in that sort of way. On the whole, yes, I'm very supportive of what you're changing. I'd far rather see this go through than it not go through. Mm-hmm. I would love to see, though, that one step more, make it even simpler- Thank-... make it even easier for small business owners to comply.

Can I just say, Mike, thank you. Just to acknowledge the things that we've heard again and again today that are workable and then the things that you think can be improved and how constructive you've been with that. So it's really helpful for our officials, and moving forward as well. So thanks for your time. Thanks for your participation. And we'll now move on to our next submitter. Thanks, Pam.
Thank you, Mike. Good luck. Thank you. We now have Paul Mackay from Business New Zealand

joining us on Zoom. Paul, can I just check that you're online? Here we are. Wonderful. Yeah. Yeah, yes. Welcome, Paul. We have 10 minutes with you for Business New Zealand, and that includes time for questions. And sorry, we hadn't acknowledged you. We have you, John, as well.

John, are you with Business New Zealand or EMA? I'm with the EMA, but we're one of the core members of Business New Zealand, and so I'm just listening in on Paul's submission. Okay, no worries. And then I'll then go on the back, and Paul will do likewise. He'll join us. So we're together. Okay. We're together, yes. Okay. Absolutely. Hence your submissions are one after the other. Right.

Yes. So Paul, over to you. You've got 10 minutes, and that includes time for questions. Thank you. Thank you very much, and I welcome this opportunity to talk to the submission. I'm Paul Mackay. I'm the Manager of Employment Relations Policy at Business New Zealand. And I have a history with the Holidays Act going back to 2003, so every review since.

We're very pleased to see this bill. It contains essentially the elements that we have always asked for, which is a simplified approach, an hours-based accrual approach. And so therefore, our submission is designed in support of the bill, but to highlight two or three things which we think, like a number of the previous submitters have said, would be different from a simplification.

And also to point out some of the issues that might arise if we can't get that kind of simplification. Some of the issues have already been covered, so I won't deal with them in detail, but I will come back to them, but I'd like to spend a little bit of time first on the remediation process.
